coredata.dc:title: An activatable nanocomposite as a hydrogen peroxide-catalytic oxygenerator for tumor imaging and enhanced photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yan
Fu, Lili
Hou, Junjun
Wang, Lingxiao
Sun, Mingzhao
Sun, Xiao
Wang, Xiaoyan
Chen, Lingxin
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy has been used in the treatment of a variety of tumors, with a high degree of tumor inhibition and reduced side effects. However, the typically hypoxic micro-environment of tumor tissue has long been a key factor limiting the efficacy of tumor photodynamic therapy. Here, we designed and synthesized a nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2, which compounded red emissive carbon-dots (RCDs) with manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2), for catalyzes hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce O2 to improve tumor hypoxic environment. The precursor of RCDs showing a wide absorption band in the visible to near-infrared region (400–800 nm) with red emission (from 500 to 800 nm, peaking at 640 nm). These prepared RCDs could produce 1O2 under 550 nm laser irradiation to achieve the purpose of photodynamic therapy. In addition, MnO2 could quench the fluorescence emission of RCDs and exhibit an RCDs@MnO2 fluorescence “off-on” function in tumor cells and tissues. RCDs@MnO2 could achieve the fluorescence imaging of tumor cells and tumor tissues, and improve the tumor hypoxic environment and enhance photodynamic treatment effect. RCDs@MnO2 has the powerful potential to be applied to fluorescence imaging of tumor tissue and significantly improve the photodynamic therapy of tumor, when investigated in vitro and in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Fluorescence Imaging
Hydrogen Peroxide
Tumor
Photodynamic Therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ability of RCDs@MnO2 to produce singlet oxygen in cells and apoptosis
RCDs@MnO2 evaluation of its tumor therapy

National Nature Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21976209, 22007005 ), the science and technology innovation development plan of Yantai of China (No. 2020MSGY113, 2020YT06000898 ), the Research Initiation Fund of Binzhou Medical University (Grant No. BY2019KYQD39, BY2020KYQD01 ), Taishan Scholar Project Special Funding (No. ts20190962 ), Shandong Peninsula Engineering Research Center of Comprehensive Brine Utilization ( 2018LS014 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The synthesis of RCDs@MnO2. b) The mechanism of RCDs@MnO2 for the detection of H2O2 and the PDT of tumor. c) Scheme of RCDs@MnO2 PDT mechanism in cells. d) Characterization of RCDs and nanocomposites RCDs@MnO2. The mean particle size distribution of RCDs. e) The TEM imaging of RCDs and f) nanocomposites RCDs@MnO2.

a) Absorption spectra of RCDs (20 µg/mL) and RCDs@MnO2 (100 µg/mL). b) The fluorescence intensity of RCDs (20 µg/mL) and RCDs@MnO2 (100 µg/mL) in different pH values (pH = 3–9). c) Fluorescence emission spectra of RCDs@MnO2 (100 µg/mL) and RCDs@MnO2 added of H2O2 (1 mM, pH = 5). d) Fluorescence intensity of RCDs@MnO2 (100 µg/mL) against the reaction time in H2O2 (1 mM). All data were obtained in PBS (pH=5, 10 mM) at 37 °C (λex = 500 nm, λem = 640 nm). e) The fluorescence data of singlet oxygen probe (SOSG): 1. RCDs@MnO2 (100 µg/mL), 2. free MnO2 nanosheets (equivalent concentrations of RCDs@MnO2) 3. free RCDs (equivalent concentrations of RCDs@MnO2)，and 4. RCDs@MnO2 (100 µg/mL) + H2O2. Fluorescence channel: λex = 490 nm, λem = 530 nm.

a) Fluorescent images and flow cytometry analysis of HepG2 cells to analyze the H2O2 by Cy-NOH2 and generated 1O2 by SOSG. b) The quantized mean fluorescence intensity of cells in a·H2O2 fluorescence channel: λex = 605 nm, λem = 700 – 800 nm. 1O2 fluorescence channel: λex = 490 nm, λem = 510 – 560 nm. Scale bar = 20 µm.

MTT assay was used to determine the cytotoxicity of a) MnO2 nanosheets, b) free RCDs and c) RCDs@MnO2 for HepG2 cells under laser light or no laser light. d) Calcein AM/PI co-stained imaging of HepG2 cells after PDT by treated with RCDs or RCDs@MnO2 by laser scanning confocal microscope. e) Apoptosis analysis of Annexin V-PE under blank, laser, RCDs or RCDs@MnO2 by flow cytometry.

Relative tumor volume (a), survival rate (b), body weight (c), H&E tumor (d) of subcutaneous HepG2 tumor xenograft mice in different groups. e) H&E staining of the normal tissues in different groups. Magnification: × 400. Data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 5).

head.ce:title: An activatable nanocomposite as a hydrogen peroxide-catalytic oxygenerator for tumor imaging and enhanced photodynamic therapy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Coastal Environmental Processes and Ecological Remediation, Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research (YIC), Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy has been used in the treatment of a variety of tumors, with a high degree of tumor inhibition and reduced side effects. However, the typically hypoxic micro-environment of tumor tissue has long been a key factor limiting the efficacy of tumor photodynamic therapy. Here, we designed and synthesized a nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2, which compounded red emissive carbon-dots (RCDs) with manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2), for catalyzes hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce O2 to improve tumor hypoxic environment. The precursor of RCDs showing a wide absorption band in the visible to near-infrared region (400–800 nm) with red emission (from 500 to 800 nm, peaking at 640 nm). These prepared RCDs could produce 1O2 under 550 nm laser irradiation to achieve the purpose of photodynamic therapy. In addition, MnO2 could quench the fluorescence emission of RCDs and exhibit an RCDs@MnO2 fluorescence “off-on” function in tumor cells and tissues. RCDs@MnO2 could achieve the fluorescence imaging of tumor cells and tumor tissues, and improve the tumor hypoxic environment and enhance photodynamic treatment effect. RCDs@MnO2 has the powerful potential to be applied to fluorescence imaging of tumor tissue and significantly improve the photodynamic therapy of tumor, when investigated in vitro and in vivo.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2 has the powerful potential to be applied to fluorescence imaging of tumor tissue and improve the photodynamic therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to conventional cancer treatment methods such as surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy [1–5], photodynamic therapy (PDT), a light-triggered strategy, has gained substantial attention both in experimental cancer study and clinical cancer practice by virtue of its reduced side effects, negligible skin phototoxicity, minimal invasiveness, minimized injury to healthy tissues, and low drug resistance [6–11]. PDT has been employed for the treatment of many tumors [12], such as Barrett’s esophagus [13], endobronchial tumors [14], bladde [15], lung [16], head and neck [17], and skin cancers [18]. In PDT, the photosensitizer (PS) molecules absorb light of the appropriate wavelength, then catalyze surrounding oxygen molecules at the tumor sites, to form reactive oxygen species (ROS), including peroxide (O2 −), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (HO•), and superoxide (O2 •−), which could oxidize cellular biomacromolecules to induce tumor ablation, vascular injury, and incite inflammation and immune response [19]. Therefore, PDT has evolved into an important therapeutic strategy for cancer [6–8]. On the basis of the machine-processed of PS-mediated ROS production, adequate oxygen supply plays an important role in the efficiency of PDT [20,21]. Unfortunately, many solid tumors are known to have a hypoxic tumor microenvironment, resulting from uncontrolled proliferation of tumor cells, irregular vascular system, and dysfunction of lymphatic system [22–26]. Therefore, the therapeutic efficacy of PDT can be markedly impaired, and it has been widely considered as a method to enhance PDT to overcome hypoxia by supplementing tumor oxygen supply. As a result of abnormal metabolic processes, solid tumors have been demonstrated to exist in a microenvironment quite different from normal tissues [27]. The glycolytic metabolism was dramatically upregulated during tumorigenesis, causing a large amount of lactic acid in these solid tumors [28], thus forming an acidic tumor microenvironment with an attractive reduce in pH value. Additionally, excess H2O2 was produced by malignant cancer cells [29] that cause a notable high level of H2O2. Thus, the microenvironment of solid tumors is acidic and rich in H2O2. In recent years, the catalytic effect of nanocomposites on hydrogen peroxide has been widely used in medical, environmental, and chemical sensing research [30,31].

Although some work has been reported, some photocatalytic strategies usually use precious metals as co-catalysts or alcohols as organic electron donors to obtain H2O2 [32], the tumor site itself has high H2O2 concentration. To take advantage of the solid tumor microenvironment, manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets are known to be reduced into Mn2+ by acidic H2O2, and generates a large amount of oxygen at the same time, that significantly improves the oxygen-dependent PDT effects on hypoxic tumors [33]. Moreover, notable biocompatibility with MnO2 nanosheets are showed due to manganese is an essential non-poisonous substance involved in physiological metabolism [34]. Consequently, MnO2 nanosheets could potentially serve as an ideal nano material to increase the oxygen content of the typically hypoxic tumor microenvironment. They could also be used as a carrier combined with photosensitive materials, synthesized as nanocomposites, to improve the efficacy of photodynamic therapy. Yet an additional advantage of MnO2 nanosheets is their utilization as an effective fluorescence quencher because of their wide absorption spectrum and large molar extinction coefficient [35] to realize “off-on” fluorescence imaging of tumor cells and tissues.

Recently, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing attention due to their exclusive characteristics, including good photostability, excellent biocompatibility, high water solubility, surface modification ﬂexibility, and efficient catalytic capability [36,37]. Although CDs have been widely utilized in sensors [38] and bioimaging [39–42], their application to PDT has seldom been investigated [43–46] owing to difficulties in preparing CDs with PDT function. Furthermore, there are few CDs with emission wavelengths that reach the near-infrared region. Considering a relative low absorption/scattering of skin, tissue, blood, and water under 650–950 nm, near-infrared (NIR) light-triggered PDT is increasingly necessary in cancer treatment [47]. Most reports use CDs as fluorescent agents due to its absorb/emit in the visible region [42]. However, little report focused on its inherent therapeutic application. Ge et al [48]. prepared CDs using polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA) as the precursor that showed a wide absorption band in the visible to NIR region (400–800 nm) with red emission (from 500 to 800 nm, peaked at 640 nm).

In the present study, in accordance with a previous report [48], we synthesized red emitting RCDs with the PDT effect of producing 1O2. MnO2 nanosheets [49] and RCDs were then combined into nanocomposites RCDs@MnO2. Demonstrated by in vitro experiments, RCDs@MnO2 demonstrated a good fluorescence “off-on” response to acidic/H2O2 conditions. MnO2 nanosheets decomposed into Mn2+, which not only catalyzed H2O2 to produce O2, but also produced a high 1O2 yield. Cellular experiments showed that RCDs@MnO2 not only produced fluorescence imaging of tumor cells, but also yielded a higher level of tumor cell apoptosis compared with single RCDs under the acidic and excessive H2O2 environment of the tumor cells and tissues. Finally, a tumor-bearing mouse model experiment showed that RCDs@MnO2 fluorescence imaging of tumor tissue could be achieved. The analysis of tumor tissue in vitro showed that RCDs@MnO2 had a definitive inhibitory effect of tumor tissue growth under laser irradiation.
The synthesis of RCDs, MnO2 Nanosheets and RCDs@MnO2 were shown in Supporting Information.

All procedures and surgical operations conformed to the National Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. This work has received approval for research ethics from Binzhou Medical University and a proof/certificate of approval is available upon request (Approval Number: 20210808–56).

The activatable behavior of RCDs@MnO2 is described in Fig. 1b and c. When the nanoplatform was in the tumor environment, MnO2 nanosheets could be decomposed into Mn2+ by H2O2 and catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 into O2 and H2O. In the above process, RCDs@MnO2 that changed to free RCDs not only displayed the precise location of the tumor by fluorescence recovery, but also achieved PDT treatment of the tumor by laser irradiation. Through transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-vis-near infrared absorption (UV–vis–NIR) and fluorescence spectroscopy, the prepared RCDs@MnO2 was characterized. The TEM image revealed that the RCDs were monodispersed and had dimensions of ∼ 3–10 nm (Fig. 1d and e). For TEM image of RCDs@MnO2, the RCDs were evenly distributed in MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 1f).

The spectroscopic properties of the nanoplatform were studied in buffer solution (10 mM PBS pH 7.4). In Fig. 2a, the band at 460 nm stared from the longitudinal absorption of the RCDs. For RCDs@MnO2 nanoplatform, the absorption peak at 460 nm disappeared due to the wide absorption spectrum and large molar extinction coefficient of MnO2 nanosheets, which also showed the nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2 formation. In Fig. 2b, the fluorescence emission intensities of single RCDs and RCDs@MnO2 were compared under different pH conditions (pH = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of RCDS was hardly affected by the change of pH values. However, the fluorescence intensity of RCDs@MnO2 was not changed when the pH value was greater than or equal to 7, but when the pH value was less than 7, the fluorescence intensity increased as the pH decreased. This showed that the MnO2 nanosheets were gradually dissolved and released RCDS with the increased acidity of the solution, resulting in an increase in the fluorescence intensity. The activation behavior was verified using fluorescence titration by adding H+ (pH = 5) and H2O2 to the RCDs@MnO2 solution. In Fig. 2c, the fluorescence intensity of RCDs@MnO2 was weak in buffer solution (10 mM PBS pH 7.4), and the maximum emission wavelength was 640 nm, about 3 × 105. However, when H2O2 was added at the same time to simulate the microenvironment of tumor cells and tissues with a pH = 5, the fluorescence emission intensity of the RCDs@MnO2 was enhanced owing to the release of RCDs, and the fluorescence intensity reached about 8 × 105. The above results showed that the fluorescence imaging function of tumor cells and tissues could be achieved through the change of fluorescence intensity of nanocomposites RCDs@MnO2 under the conditions of the tumor microenvironment.

The chemical reaction kinetics of RCDs@MnO2 under the simulated tumor microenvironment was also one of the very important factors to realize fluorescence imaging. Therefore, we studied the fluorescence change curve (180 s) of RCDs@MnO2 after adding buffer solution (pH = 5) and H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2d, the fluorescence signal of RCDs@MnO2 increased immediately after adding the simulated buffer solution, and reached the peak after about 60 s, and remained unchanged over 180 s. All the above experimental results showed that the nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2 possessed good fluorescence properties and had the potential for cellular and in vivo research because it could achieve rapid and sensitive fluorescence intensity changes in the tumor microenvironment.

Based on the good fluorescence properties of RCDs@MnO2, we further studied the singlet oxygen production ability of RCDs@MnO2 in vitro to investigate whether it improved the PDT efficiency. Under conditions simulating tumor microenvironment (pH = 5), the singlet oxygen signals (1O2) generated from the RCDs@MnO2 (550 nm laser at 1.5 W/cm2 in the presence or absence of H2O2) were compared by using the singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG, Invitrogen co., USA). In Fig. 2e, contrasted to the RCDs@MnO2, free MnO2 nanosheets (equivalent concentrations of RCDs@MnO2), free RCDs (equivalent concentrations of RCDs@MnO2), and RCDs@MnO2 + H2O2, RCDs@MnO2 and free MnO2 showed almost no fluorescence signal for SOSG, and free RCDs and RCDs@MnO2 + H2O2 displayed a strong fluorescence signal for SOSG using the fluorescence spectrometer. The fluorescence emission spectra of SOSG indicated that 1O2 was generated via RCDs in the solution. However, the 1O2 production of free RCDs was less than the RCDs@MnO2 + H2O2 group. This result showed that the nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2 could effectively catalyze H2O2 to produce O2, and convert O2 to 1O2 under laser irradiation, with good 1O2 yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ability of RCDs@MnO2 to produce singlet oxygen in cells and apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Imaging of intracellular H2O2 and detection of H2O2 in different cell lines with RCDs@MnO2 were shown in Fig. S2 and S3. To investigate whether RCDs@MnO2 could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into O2 after adding cells, we used the previously synthesized H2O2 fluorescent probe (Cy-NOH2) [50] to analyze cell imaging. Concurrently, the production of singlet oxygen under laser irradiation was detected by commercial singlet oxygen fluorescence probe (SOSG). Prior to imaging, HepG2 cells in control group were incubated with H2O2 fluorescent probe and SOSG for 30 min. In the RCDs@MnO2 group, cells were incubated with RCDs@MnO2 for 8 h, then incubated with H2O2 fluorescent probe and SOSG for 30 min. In RCDs@MnO2 + Laser group, cells were incubated with RCDs@MnO2 for 8 h, then laser irradiation for 5 min, followed by H2O2 fluorescent probe and SOSG incubation for 30 min. The imaging results of laser confocal microscope are shown in Fig. 3. Due to the large amount of H2O2 in tumor cells, strong fluorescence signals were observed in the H2O2 channel of control group cells, and almost no fluorescence signals were observed in 1O2 channel, indicating that 1O2 was not produced. We found that adding only RCDs@MnO2 without laser irradiation significantly reduced the H2O2 content, while the group with laser irradiation simultaneously produced a large amount of 1O2. The above experimental results were consistent with the analysis results of flow cytometry (Fig. 3). RCDs@MnO2 could effectively catalyze excessive H2O2 in tumor cells to produce O2. At the same time, RCDS could effectively convert O2 into 1O2 under laser irradiation to kill tumors cells.

Further, we used MTT (a measure of cellular metabolic activity—indicator of cell viability) to analyze the cell survival rate under different conditions. The cells were divided into three groups. To the first group of cells were added different concentrations of MnO2; to the second group were added different concentrations of RCDS, and to the third group were added different concentrations of RCDs@MnO2. All cells were incubated with nano-materials for 8 h. As shown in Fig. 4a-c, the cells had different concentrations of MnO2, and the cell survival rate remained above 90% with or without laser irradiation, indicating that MnO2 had low cytotoxicity. Different concentrations of RCDS were added to the cells, and the cell survival rate remained above 90% without laser irradiation. However, under laser irradiation, the cell survival rate decreased with increasing concentrations of RCDS, with a cell survival rate of only 32% at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. This suggested that RCDS could change the oxygen in cells into 1O2 with the addition of laser irradiation, which greatly increases the apoptosis of cells. Cells were added with different concentrations of RCDs@MnO2 in the absence of laser irradiation, and the cell survival rate also remained above 90%, indicating that our RCDs@MnO2 has good biocompatibility. However, the cell survival rate decreased with increasing concentrations of RCDs@MnO2 and the addition of laser irradiation. This further confirmed that RCDS converted intracellular O2 into 1O2, which greatly increased cell apoptosis. However, the cell survival rate was much lower than the RCDS alone because the RCDs@MnO2 catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 into O2, increasing the cellular content of O2, which in turn was converted into 1O2 by RCDS resulting in a cell survival rate close to 0%. The above experimental results show that the RCDs@MnO2 greatly increased the apoptosis rate of tumor cells and enhanced the PDT effect on the tumor cells.

Through co-stained fluorescence result of propidium iodide (PI) and Calcine AM, the PDT of RCDs@MnO2 in HepG2 cells was demonstrated (Fig. 4d). The apoptosis rate of HepG2 cells was also analyzed by an apoptosis kit (Annexin V-PE) (Fig. 4e). The apoptosis rate of RCDs@MnO2 group was much higher than either the single RCDs or single laser. At the same time, the results of flow cytometry analysis of apoptosis were consistent with the above experimental results. The above experiments verified that the RCDs@MnO2 had the most effective results of tumor cell PDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RCDs@MnO2 evaluation of its tumor therapy
ce:section.ce:para: RCDs@MnO2 in vivo distribution (BALB/c mice and HepG2 tumor model) were shown in Fig. S6. In view of the outstanding singlet oxygen yield of RCDs@MnO2 plus its intense and prolonged accumulation in the tumor site, RCDs@MnO2 was investigated for treatment of the HepG2 tumor xenograft models. Four groups were randomly divided: 1) PBS nonirradiation group, the tumor xenograft models were injected (i.v.) with PBS buffer solution without laser irradiation; 2) BS irradiation group, similar to PBS nonirradiation group but given 550 nm NIR laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2) for 5 min; 3) RCDs irradiation group, RCDs (5 mg/kg) were injected into tumor xenograft models with laser irradiation; 4) RCDs@MnO2 irradiation group, similar to RCDs irradiation group but given 550 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2) for 5 min. To study the effect of RCDs@MnO2 for anti-tumor, the survival rate and relative tumor volume (every 2 d after irradiation) were recorded. Ten-fold increase of the relative tumor volumes was exhibited in the PBS nonirradiation group and PBS irradiation group, which indicated that no effect of the single laser irradiation on tumor growth ( Fig. 5a) RCDs irradiation group showed a faint change of tumor growth, attributed to the hypoxic tumor microenvironment that led to a small amount singlet oxygen yield (Fig. 5a). Notably, an obvious tumor inhibition was showed in the RCDs@MnO2 irradiation group (Fig. 5a), and the survival rate of different groups was shown in Fig. 5b, demonstrating that an effective PDT of RCDs@MnO2 for tumor.

The overall health status of the models was also recorded through body weight (Fig. 5c), which suggested excellent biocompatibility and only modest side effects of RCDs@MnO2. To further analyze the anti-tumor effect of RCDs@MnO2, the post-treatment tumor-damage of tumor tissue was observed through hematoxylin & eosin (H&E) staining. Gross damage of tumor tissue was demonstrated in the RCDs@MnO2 irradiation group by H&E staining (Fig. 5d), indicating excellent efficacy. Nevertheless, minimal tumor damage inhibited in the PBS nonirradiation group, PBS irradiation group and RCDs irradiation group. Of major importance, no distinct damage for normal tissues was caused by RCDs@MnO2 irradiation such as the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney (Fig. 5e). Therefore, it is reassuring RCDs@MnO2 provided efficient PDT to the tumor without obvious side effect.

In summary, we designed and synthesized a nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2 that has “off-on” fluorescence response under the conditions of tumor microenvironment, due to the wide absorption spectrum of MnO2. In addition, RCDs@MnO2 could also catalyze H2O2 in tumor cells and tissues to produce O2, improving the hypoxic microenvironment of tumor and PDT of the tumor. The experimental results showed that RCDs@MnO2 had obvious fluorescence response and O2 generation under conditions simulating the tumor microenvironment (acid / H2O2) in vitro. The results of cell experiments showed that RCDs@MnO2 had low biological toxicity. In addition, compared with normal cells, RCDs@MnO2 had stronger fluorescence signal in tumor cells. Under laser irradiation, the nanocomposite RCDs@MnO2 had stronger tumor-killing ability compared with single RCDs.

Yan Huang: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Lili Fu: Conceptualization, Software, Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Junjun Hou: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Lingxiao Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Mingzhao Sun: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Xiao Sun: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Xiaoyan Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Lingxin Chen: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21976209, 22007005), the science and technology innovation development plan of Yantai of China (No. 2020MSGY113, 2020YT06000898), the Research Initiation Fund of Binzhou Medical University (Grant No. BY2019KYQD39, BY2020KYQD01), Taishan Scholar Project Special Funding (No. ts20190962), Shandong Peninsula Engineering Research Center of Comprehensive Brine Utilization (2018LS014).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133663.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A smart photosensitizer-manganese dioxide nanosystem for enhanced photodynamic therapy by reducing glutathione levels in cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fan H., Yan G., Zhao Z., Hu X., Zhang W., Liu H., Fu X., Fu T., Zhang X.B., Tan W.: A Smart Photosensitizer-Manganese Dioxide Nanosystem for Enhanced Photodynamic Therapy by Reducing Glutathione Levels in Cancer Cells. Angewandte Chemie 2016, 55(18):5477–5482.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Specific light-up bioprobe with aggregation-induced emission and activatable photoactivity for the targeted and image-guided photodynamic ablation of cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuan Y., Zhang C.J., Gao M., Zhang R., Tang B.Z., Liu B.: Specific light-up bioprobe with aggregation-induced emission and activatable photoactivity for the targeted and image-guided photodynamic ablation of cancer cells. Angewandte Chemie 2015, 54(6):1780–1786.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Designing red-light-activated multifunctional agents for the photodynamic therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Higgins S.L., Brewer K.J.: Designing red-light-activated multifunctional agents for the photodynamic therapy. Angewandte Chemie 2012, 51(46):11420–11422.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric biosensor for aggregation-induced emission-guided precise photodynamic therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Han K., Wang S.B., Lei Q., Zhu J.Y., Zhang X.Z.: Ratiometric Biosensor for Aggregation-Induced Emission-Guided Precise Photodynamic Therapy. ACS nano 2015, 9(10):10268–10277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scintillating nanoparticles as energy mediators for enhanced photodynamic therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluate the bisphenol A-induced redox state in cells, zebrafish and in vivo with a hydrogen peroxide turn-on fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Huang Y., Yu L., Lu P., Wei Y., Fu L., Hou J., Wang Y., Wang X., Chen L.: Evaluate the bisphenol A-induced redox state in cells, zebrafish and in vivo with a hydrogen peroxide turn-on fluorescent probe. Journal of hazardous materials 2022, 424: 127425.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yan Huang is currently as a professor at School of Pharmacy, Binzhou Medical University. He received his Ph.D., joint-educated, at Shandong Normal University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2019. His research interests focus on functional fluorescent probe, theranostics, and functional nanomaterial.

Lili Fu is currently a master candidate, joint-educated, at Binzhou Medical University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2020. Her current interest in research focuses on fluorescence bioimaging.

Junjun Hou is currently a master candidate, joint-educated, at Binzhou Medical University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2020. Her current interest in research focuses on fluorescence bioimaging.

Lingxiao Wang is currently a master candidate, joint-educated, at Binzhou Medical University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2021. Her current interest in research focuses on fluorescence bioimaging.

Mingzhao Sun is currently a master candidate, joint-educated, at Binzhou Medical University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2021. His current interest in research focuses on bioimaging.

Xiao Sun is currently a master candidate, joint-educated, at Binzhou Medical University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2022. Her current interest in research focuses on fluorescence bioimaging.

Xiaoyan Wang is currently as a professor at School of Pharmacy, Binzhou Medical University. She received her Ph.D., joint-educated, at Shandong Normal University, and Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2017. Her research interests focus on the studies of functionalized nanoparticles for developing nanoscale biochemical analysis methods and molecular imprinting-based sample pretreatment technology.

Lingxin Chen has been a professor at Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2009. He obtained his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2003. During 2004–2009, he worked at Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, and Department of Applied Chemistry, Hanyang University, respectively. His research interests include the studies of novel properties of materials such as functionalized nanoparticles & functional probe molecules for developing nanoscale biochemical analysis methods and molecular imprinting-based sample pretreatment technology.
